header.title: Daily FI Tape
header.subtitle: The June 3 close was a real-yield-led rates selloff. Oil and inflation risk outweighed any comfort from a forthcoming jobs report, listed credit funds weakened, and the day's reporting showed a global policy map that was moving toward restraint rather than synchronized easing.
header.as_of_note: The June 3 close was a real-yield-led rates selloff. Oil and inflation risk outweighed any comfort from a forthcoming jobs report, listed credit funds weakened, and the day's reporting showed a global policy map that was moving toward restraint rather than synchronized easing.

overall_summary.headline: Real yields, oil, and DXY firmness made June 3 the hardest rates-negative close in the window.
bullets: UST yields rose 2-4 bp across the curve.
10Y TIPS +4 bp and breakevens -1 bp made it real-yield-led.
WTI, Brent, DXY, and VIX rose; gold fell.
Listed credit funds fell, but selective issuance stayed open.

reader_takeaways.label: NY Close
reader_takeaways.title: Real yields drove the selloff
reader_takeaways.summary: Nominal yields rose while breakevens fell, so the rates damage came through real-rate compensation.

reader_takeaways.label: Policy
reader_takeaways.title: Oil and Fed risk kept pressure on
reader_takeaways.summary: Higher crude, a firmer dollar, and policy-credibility risk made the selloff harder than a growth scare.

reader_takeaways.label: Credit
reader_takeaways.title: Issuance was open but selective
reader_takeaways.summary: listed-fund weakness sat beside Bahrain, loan, and project-finance activity, keeping borrower quality central.

overall_summary.ny_close_causal_narrative: The NY close was a real-yield-led rates selloff: jobs and Fed-path sensitivity lifted real yields, oil and DXY firmness confirmed policy pressure, and credit stayed open only for better-compensated or stronger borrowers.

top_drivers.title: Real yields drove the Treasury selloff
top_drivers.short_summary: UST10Y rose 3 bp to 4.49%, UST5Y rose 4 bp to 4.21%, and 10Y TIPS rose 4 bp while 10Y breakevens slipped 1 bp.
top_drivers.detail: The selloff was not an inflation-breakeven surge. It was closer to a real-rate repricing: nominal yields rose, 10Y TIPS moved up by more than the nominal 10Y, and the curve stayed broadly controlled. The day's reporting tied the move to a jobs-gauge reaction, oil-linked inflation concern, and options demand for higher 10-year yields.
top_drivers.market_move: UST10Y rose 3 bp to 4.49% and 10Y TIPS rose 4 bp to 2.11% while 10Y breakevens fell 1 bp.
top_drivers.transmission: The market demanded more real return for duration as jobs, oil, and Fed-path risk raised the policy hurdle.
top_drivers.confirmation: The curve stayed controlled, so the move was a real-yield repricing rather than a disorderly long-end liquidation.

top_drivers.title: Oil and policy risk overruled soft-growth comfort
top_drivers.short_summary: WTI rose 2.41% to 96.02, Brent rose 1.89% to 97.81, DXY rose 0.31%, and VIX rose 1.84%.
top_drivers.detail: Cross-asset pricing was consistent with a market that had returned to inflation vigilance. Fed commentary, Beige Book energy-cost references, and a dollar-sensitive Warsh debut framed the June 16-17 meeting as a risk event. EM and Japan policy stories also showed that energy-driven inflation was forcing central banks to defend credibility.
top_drivers.market_move: WTI rose 2.41%, Brent rose 1.89%, DXY rose 0.31%, and VIX rose 1.84% while SPX and NDX declined.
top_drivers.transmission: Oil and DXY firmness turned policy credibility into a cross-asset constraint on rates and risk appetite.
top_drivers.confirmation: Gold fell and SOX rose, narrowing the read to real-rate pressure rather than a full haven scramble.

top_drivers.title: Credit weakened at the margin but issuance was not closed
top_drivers.short_summary: LQD fell 0.28%, HYG fell 0.28%, JNK fell 0.22%, and EMB fell 0.37%, while Bahrain still cleared a dollar bond and leveraged financing remained active.
top_drivers.detail: Credit was softer, but not frozen. Bahrain sold 1 billion dollars of bonds after a missile incident and tightened from initial price talk, while Belden-related loan financing and Canadian project debt showed primary-market activity. At the same time, software refinancing anxiety, bank and nonbank delinquency concerns, Zambia holdout risk and weaker ETF total returns made the credit read selective rather than broadly positive.
top_drivers.market_move: LQD, HYG, JNK, and EMB fell, while Bahrain, leveraged financing, and project debt still cleared selectively.
top_drivers.transmission: Higher real yields and credit scrutiny lifted the compensation required for weaker or complex borrowers.
top_drivers.confirmation: Primary-market activity confirmed access remained open, but listed-fund weakness and borrower-specific stress argued against broad spread comfort.

latest_asia_premarket.summary: Separately, latest Asia/pre-market was mixed versus the NY close.

latest_asia_premarket.market_implication: 10Y note fut -0.17% fades duration; S&P fut -0.4% fades risk.

watch_events.title: US payroll and wage mix
watch_events.watch_line: Payrolls and wages need to show whether real-yield pressure was justified.
watch_events.market_trigger: Payrolls or wages surprise firm enough to keep Fed hike optionality alive.
watch_events.market_implication: A firm labor mix would keep real yields elevated and challenge duration.
watch_events.why_it_matters: The close priced inflation sensitivity more than labor weakness; payrolls and wages will test whether that hierarchy holds.
watch_events.confirms_if: Labor data are soft enough to reduce real-yield pressure without implying recession stress.
watch_events.challenges_if: Firm payrolls or wages revive a higher-for-longer Fed path.

watch_events.title: Fed statement repricing
watch_events.watch_line: Statement and projections risk can move both front-end rates and FX.
watch_events.market_trigger: A statement or projections surprise keeps hikes live or shifts political-risk assumptions.
watch_events.market_implication: A hawkish surprise would pressure front-end rates, support DXY, and tighten hard-currency funding.
watch_events.why_it_matters: Any Warsh-era statement change can move both front-end rates and FX because the market is debating whether hikes remain live.
watch_events.confirms_if: Fed communication does not add new hike optionality and DXY stabilizes.
watch_events.challenges_if: Fed language or projections lift hike odds and the dollar together.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: UST2Y, DXY, EUR, JPY, AUD, NZD, and EM hard-currency spreads.

watch_events.title: Credit primary clearing levels
watch_events.watch_line: New debt must clear with enough demand to offset weaker listed-fund tone.
watch_events.market_trigger: Deals need larger concessions or lower-quality borrowers lose access.
watch_events.market_implication: Weak clearing would turn the day from selective softness into broader financing pressure.
watch_events.why_it_matters: High demand for new debt alongside weaker listed funds would confirm selectivity rather than a closed credit window.
watch_events.confirms_if: Deals clear with strong books and stable concessions despite weaker listed funds.
watch_events.challenges_if: Concessions widen or deals are delayed as real yields rise.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: HY spreads, AT1, leveraged loans, project debt, and EM sovereign bonds.

cross_asset_cards.title: Oil
cross_asset_cards.summary: WTI +2.41% day, +8.28% week; Brent +1.89%. 10Y breakevens fell 1 bp, so inflation compensation diverged.

cross_asset_cards.title: Equities
cross_asset_cards.summary: SPX -0.74%, NDX -0.29%, SOX +1.39%; VIX +1.84%; semiconductor exception.

cross_asset_cards.title: Dollar / gold
cross_asset_cards.summary: DXY rose 0.31% while gold fell 1.17%; Treasury yields were higher; tighter financial conditions.

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The June 3 close was the clearest rates-negative day in this three-day window. Treasuries sold off across the curve: UST2Y rose 3 bp to 4.08%, UST5Y rose 4 bp to 4.21%, UST10Y rose 3 bp to 4.49%, and UST30Y rose 2 bp to 4.99%. The move was not a classic breakeven-led inflation scare. TIPS10Y rose 4 bp to 2.11%, while 10Y breakevens slipped 1 bp to 2.38%. That means the day was better described as a real-rate and policy-reaction repricing, with the market demanding more real compensation even as inflation compensation itself did not widen.

The market evidence fit that price action. A Treasury-market story tied the 10-year yield increase to a jobs gauge, oil prices, and options activity that included a buyer targeting a move toward a 4.7% 10-year yield by late July. The evidence also made clear that traders were less focused on the mechanical surprise potential of payrolls and more focused on whether inflation pressure would force the Fed to keep policy restrictive. That is exactly the kind of backdrop in which real yields can rise even when breakevens do not.

Cross-asset confirmation was harsher than on June 2. WTI rose 2.41% to 96.02 and Brent rose 1.89% to 97.81. DXY rose 0.31%, gold fell 1.17%, SPX fell 0.74%, NDX fell 0.29%, and VIX rose 1.84%. SOX was still higher, so the equity market was not a one-way liquidation, but the broader signal was that higher energy prices and a stronger dollar were pressing against risk appetite. Listed credit funds also weakened: LQD and HYG both fell 0.28%, JNK fell 0.22%, and EMB fell 0.37%. Because those are total-return instruments, they combine rates and spread effects, but the direction was still consistent with a tougher fixed-income close.

The global policy mix made the global dimension important. Emerging-market central banks were described as leading a rate-hike wave because the Iran war had reignited inflation. Japan was preparing for a possible BOJ hike, with traders assigning high odds to a June move. Australia showed weak GDP but still faced a central bank that had already lifted rates back to 4.35%. Poland and Ghana had more nuanced stories, but they still revolved around the same question: can central banks treat the energy shock as temporary, or do they have to defend inflation credibility?

The day therefore cannot be written as a UST-only selloff. The Treasury curve was the pricing anchor, but global policy, oil, FX, and credit all reinforced the same message. Markets were not rejecting every risky asset, and primary markets were still open, but the burden of proof had shifted. Investors needed evidence that inflation was slowing or that growth was weak enough to restrain central banks. Until then, the default bias was higher real rates and more selectivity in credit.

## UST Rates, Curve, Fed Pricing, Supply, Term Premium, And Liquidity

The curve details tell the story. UST2Y rose 3 bp, UST5Y rose 4 bp, UST10Y rose 3 bp, and UST30Y rose 2 bp. 2s10s stayed at 41 bp, while 2s30s flattened 1 bp to 91 bp. The belly underperformed slightly, consistent with a market repricing the Fed path and intermediate real-rate compensation rather than simply adding long-end term premium. The 2s5s10s butterfly moved 2 bp less negative to -15 bp, also consistent with belly pressure.

The TIPS and breakeven split is the central rates fact. If the day were primarily about higher inflation compensation, 10Y breakevens should have widened. Instead, TIPS10Y rose 4 bp and BE10Y fell 1 bp. That points to a real-yield-led selloff. It says investors were demanding more real return to hold duration, not merely repricing inflation expectations higher. In practice, that is often a more difficult environment for equities and credit because higher real yields tighten discount rates and reduce the room for weak borrowers to refinance cheaply.

The Fed reporting explains why real yields mattered. Williams said there was no obvious direction for rates, with inflation high and energy, tariffs and AI investment all adding pressure, even as services inflation had slowed. Logan said a hike might be needed later in the year if inflation did not move back toward target. The Beige Book reported that energy-related costs tied to the Middle East were pushing inflation across districts, with spillovers into shipping, groceries, packaging and fertilizers. Bessent's attempt to describe inflation as a short-term blip did not remove the market's concern that central banks might have to react if second-round effects appeared.

The Warsh factor added another layer. The evidence said the June 16-17 Fed meeting could move the dollar and FX more than markets were pricing, especially if the statement or projections changed. Swaps pricing put the probability of a quarter-point hike by year-end around three-quarters in that discussion. The same rates discussion noted that a different inflation gauge favored by Warsh could argue for lower Treasury yields over time, but that was not the close's dominant signal. On June 3, the market traded the immediate risk that inflation and energy would keep policy restrictive.

The options activity matters because it turned the rates debate into a price level. A large buyer of puts targeting a 10-year yield near 4.7% by late July showed that the market was not only discussing higher-for-longer in prose; it was paying for upside yield risk. With UST10Y already near 4.49%, that target was close enough to matter for convexity, risk limits and hedging behavior.

The rates conclusion is straightforward: June 3 was a real-rate selloff driven by inflation vigilance and Fed-path uncertainty. The curve did not steepen dramatically, breakevens did not widen, and the long end did not become the sole problem. The belly and real-yield components carried the burden. The better rates read therefore starts with Fed pricing, TIPS, oil and options rather than a generic Treasury selloff label.

The municipal and project-finance channel also mattered. Alphabet's involvement in a one billion dollar prepaid energy municipal transaction showed that large technology-linked demand for tax-exempt and infrastructure-style financing was moving beyond standard corporate bonds. Quincy, by contrast, illustrated how leverage constrains public-sector borrowers: a proposed purchase of Eastern Nazarene College required new municipal borrowing while the city already carried a heavy debt load. Those two facts show how the same rates backdrop can support sophisticated financing for strong counterparties while making local-government balance sheets more fragile.

Australia and India together gave a clean cross-market liquidity read. Australia had strong auction demand because investors believed policy was near a pause and wanted medium-term bonds. India had companies skipping bond issuance because local yields had risen and bank credit was easier to use. One is duration demand; the other is balance-sheet substitution. Both are valid supply facts, but they imply different risks for the US reader: demand can return quickly where yields are attractive, while borrowers under rate pressure may quietly migrate away from term markets.

The direct market-function evidence on June 3 was narrower than the rates evidence, and it was mostly outside the US Treasury close. The strongest positive supply signal came from Australia, where demand for seven-year government debt surged as investors increasingly bet the RBA tightening cycle was nearing an end. Investors sought more than four times the A$1 billion on offer, and the bid-to-cover ratio was the strongest for that maturity since the previous November. That is legitimate auction-demand evidence.

The Australian auction also helps interpret the global curve backdrop. Australia's GDP slowed sharply to 0.3% in the first quarter, with government spending weaker and households squeezed by inflation and rate hikes. Yet business investment was strong, led by data centers and machinery. A weak growth print plus a still-restrictive central bank created demand for the belly of the government curve, because investors could buy a slowdown hedge at yields that had already repriced. That is different from the US close, where real yields rose and the market still worried about inflation.

India gave a different supply and liquidity signal. Bank credit growth accelerated to 16.2%, the fastest pace since 2024, while local bond sales fell 11%. Companies were choosing bank loans or credit lines over bond issuance because sovereign yields had climbed and corporate borrowing costs had risen. That is a crowding and transmission story: higher benchmark yields can push borrowers away from capital markets and into bank balance sheets. It does not tell us that US Treasury supply was hard to absorb, but it shows how rate pressure changes funding behavior in another large market.

Zambia also belongs in the supply section because it was trying to buy back a bond before a costly coupon step-up, but a blocking bondholder group forced negotiations. That is a sovereign liability-management issue rather than a broad duration driver. It shows why high coupons and restructuring clauses matter when global rates are high: the cost of missing a window is not only a market-price move, but a legal and cash-flow problem.

The US term-premium point should be kept clean. A higher UST10Y and UST30Y close is not, by itself, evidence about auction demand or dealer capacity. The evidence did not provide a direct US auction stress signal for this date. What it did provide was a market paying for higher 10-year yields, a Fed debate that kept front-end risk alive, and global examples of how higher rates affect issuance and funding choice. That is enough to make the supply section useful without overstating it.

The conclusion is that liquidity and supply were supportive only where direct evidence said so. Australia cleared well. India showed corporate substitution away from bonds. Zambia showed the cost of missing debt-management timing. The US Treasury selloff should remain a rates and policy story, with term-premium risk present but not proven by the close alone.

## Credit Conditions: IG, HY, Issuance, Refinancing, And Spread Confirmation

The software and direct-lending comments sharpened the borrower-quality split. Lenders were becoming more selective around software because AI could challenge existing cash-flow durability, and some direct lenders were described as bidding only when pricing was compelling enough. That is not a shutdown of direct lending. It is a move from abundant capital to harsher underwriting, with sponsors trying to demonstrate that their portfolio companies have defensive and offensive AI strategies rather than simply hoping growth solves leverage.

The Fed's supervision reporting reinforced that caution. Bank loan delinquencies had risen slightly across several categories, and regulators were paying attention to direct-lending and nonbank defaults. That does not mean systemic credit stress was present on June 3, but it explains why higher real yields matter. If loan growth is steady but collateral management is being revisited, then the market is not just pricing coupons; it is reassessing the margin of safety behind nonbank exposures.

Credit was worse on the margin than the previous day. LQD, HYG, JNK and EMB all fell, and the rate component explains part of that weakness. The important point is not to infer spread widening mechanically from those total returns, but the broader evidence still shows a more cautious credit environment. Higher real yields, weaker equities outside semiconductors, and a stronger dollar are not friendly to leveraged balance sheets.

The credit facts were selective rather than uniformly negative. Bahrain issued 1 billion dollars of bonds just after intercepting Iranian missiles. It priced 10-year notes at 7.125% versus initial talk around 7.5%, and orders peaked above 3.2 billion dollars. That is a strong access signal for a sovereign borrower in the region most directly linked to the geopolitical shock. It says investors were still willing to take risk when the coupon, spread and sovereign story were attractive.

At the same time, direct-lending and leveraged credit carried clear warnings. The evidence highlighted Fed concern about slightly higher bank loan delinquencies and the need for banks to revisit collateral practices tied to nonbank exposures. Software debt was described as facing a stress test because AI can weaken legacy cash-flow assumptions, and direct lending lenders were becoming more selective. DoubleLine warned that AI debt could eventually reach bubble-like conditions, even while demand for current deals remained strong and hyperscaler backing still helped many financings.

Primary-market activity remained visible. JPMorgan was preparing a leveraged loan financing for Belden's Ruckus acquisition. Coastal GasLink was readying a Canadian bond sale. Google parent Alphabet was tied to a prepaid energy municipal transaction. These examples show that funding markets were not shut. But they do not prove easy credit. They show that investors were willing to underwrite discrete structures, especially where collateral, sponsor or essential-service features were clear.

The weakest credit signals came from refinancing and sovereign debt-management problems. Zambia's blocked buyback mattered because a coupon jump from 0.5% to 7.5% could become expensive if the government meets the relevant IMF metric. India showed companies shifting away from bonds toward bank lending. Quincy and other municipal or public-sector names showed how debt burdens can constrain fiscal choices. These are small relative to the US Treasury curve, but they are exactly the kinds of names that become more fragile when real yields rise.

The credit conclusion is therefore selective deterioration, not a freeze. The close punished duration and lifted real yields. ETFs were weaker. Yet Bahrain cleared a deal, leveraged financing was still being marketed, and EM winners such as Ghana and Hungary had positive narratives. The right read is a higher hurdle rate: investors were still open for business, but only after sorting by coupon, collateral, policy credibility and exposure to higher real rates.

The credit conclusion should therefore be borrower- and instrument-specific. LQD, HYG, JNK, and EMB all fell and correctly captured the rates-plus-risk pressure, but they did not by themselves prove broad spread decompression. Bahrain, Belden-related loan financing, Canadian project debt, software-refinancing anxiety, delinquency monitoring, Zambia holdout risk, and direct-lending selectivity gave the sharper hierarchy: primary markets remained open, but investors demanded better compensation or stronger borrower stories.

## DM Global Duration

Japan was a central global-duration signal. Ueda's remarks increased confidence that the BOJ could raise rates at the June meeting if inflation risk outweighed the growth shock, and traders were assigning high odds to a quarter-point move. At the same time, Japan had just spent a record amount supporting the yen and authorized household support while keeping calendar issuance unchanged. That combination links inflation, FX defense, fiscal optics and bond supply more tightly than a standard policy-preview story.

Japan was one of the most important non-US duration stories. Ueda indicated a good chance of a June hike if inflation risks outweighed the growth hit from the Middle East crisis. Traders were assigning high odds to a quarter-point move that would take the benchmark to the highest level since the mid-1990s. Japan also disclosed a record month of yen support between late April and late May, and authorized an extra budget to cushion households from inflation while keeping overall issuance unchanged on a calendar basis. That mix links FX defense, inflation, fiscal optics and bond supply in a way that matters for global duration.

Australia showed the opposite side of the same problem. GDP was weak, but inflation and prior hikes still constrained the RBA. The bond auction showed demand for medium-term debt as investors priced slowdown risk and a possible pause. This is not inconsistent with the US selloff. It means global duration markets were trading different points on the inflation-growth tradeoff. The US close emphasized real yields and Fed risk. Australia emphasized slowdown and peak-rate risk. Japan emphasized credibility and FX.

## EM Rates, FX, And Hard-Currency Credit

Ghana and Hungary showed the more constructive side of EM. Ghana raised its growth forecast, cited stronger reserves and lower inflation than a year earlier, even though another report showed inflation rising again in May. Hungary benefited from political change, potential EU funds and rate-cut expectations. These stories were not broad EM beta; they were reform and credibility trades. They stand in contrast to Zambia's bondholder dispute and the weaker credit examples elsewhere in the day.

India's plan to attract more foreign bond investment also mattered for EM flows. Reducing taxes and easing ownership caps can improve foreign demand for government securities, but it comes while the rupee is weak and the global inflation shock is forcing central banks to defend credibility. The implication is that EM duration demand depends on both policy access and currency stability. A stronger dollar on June 3 made that balance more difficult.

The global section was unusually important because the inflation shock was not just a US story. Emerging-market central banks were described as leading a rate-hike wave as war-related commodity and food pressures complicated inflation. That changed the usual hierarchy: many developed-market central banks were waiting to assess the growth hit, while several EM central banks felt they had to defend inflation credibility and currencies more aggressively.

EM hard-currency credit was mixed. Ghana raised its growth forecast, pointed to lower inflation than a year earlier, and had stronger reserves, but another Ghana inflation story showed May prices rising again under the Iran shock. Hungary bonds were supported by political change, expected EU aid and possible cuts. Bahrain showed market access despite regional conflict. Zambia showed holdout risk. This is why EM should not be reduced to a single beta call. Investors were sorting reform credibility, commodity exposure, external financing, and coupon step-up risk.

## Cross-Asset Confirmation

Cross-asset pricing confirmed the harder rates environment. Oil rose, the DXY firmnessened, gold fell, and VIX rose. SPX and NDX declined, though SOX held up. That combination fits a real-rate shock more than a growth panic. The market did not flee every risk asset, but the higher discount-rate channel was visible. Listed credit funds moved lower and EM beta softened.

The checked board reinforced that hard-rates read. SPX fell 0.74%, NDX fell 0.29%, VIX rose 1.84%, DXY rose 0.31%, WTI rose 2.41%, Brent rose 1.89%, gold fell 1.17%, and the listed credit-fund gauges declined. SOX was the exception, rising 1.39%, which kept the tape from becoming a full growth panic. Cross-asset confirmation therefore narrowed the story to real rates, oil, DXY firmness, and selective credit pressure rather than a generalized liquidation. The SOX exception matters because it shows AI equity leadership could survive the day even while the broader fixed-income discount-rate channel tightened.

## Market Significance And Watchpoints

A further watchpoint is whether municipal and infrastructure financing remains insulated from the real-yield move. Alphabet-linked prepaid energy finance, Coastal GasLink-style project debt and other essential-service structures can clear even when broader credit softens. If those deals require larger concessions, the pressure has moved from speculative credit into higher-quality long-duration assets.

Another watchpoint is dollar sensitivity around the Fed meeting. The Warsh-risk discussion identified the euro, yen and Australian and New Zealand dollars as vulnerable to a surprise in the statement or projections. That matters for hard-currency credit because a stronger dollar tightens external funding conditions even when local reform stories remain constructive.

Payrolls are the first validation point: whether the June 3 real-yield selloff extends toward the 4.7% 10-year level targeted in options activity. That is close enough to affect mortgages, investment-grade duration, and equity multiples. A move in that direction with stable breakevens would keep the pressure on real rates and risk assets. A reversal driven by weaker payrolls or softer inflation would show that the market had overpaid for Fed risk.

Fed communication is the second validation point: payroll composition. The evidence emphasized that traders were focused on inflation more than employment, but the jobs report still matters. A strong payroll or wage print would make the Fed's conundrum worse, because it would support demand while energy is lifting price pressure. A soft print would help duration only if it is weak enough to overcome inflation concern.

Oil is the third validation point. The June 3 close had oil up, DXY up, gold down and real yields higher. If oil continues higher and breakevens still do not widen, the market is treating energy as a central-bank credibility problem rather than just a compensation trade. If breakevens begin widening with oil, the inflation problem becomes more visible and harder for the front end to ignore.

Credit access is the fourth validation point. Bahrain's deal showed that investors would buy risk when compensation was attractive. Software refinancing concerns, direct-lending selectivity, and Zambia's holdout problem showed where fragility sits. The next test is whether new debt continues to clear with tighter pricing, or whether issuers have to pay up as real yields rise.

Global policy dispersion is the fifth validation point. Japan and EM central banks were closer to hikes, Australia had a slowdown and auction-demand story, and the UK and Poland were more nuanced. If dispersion widens, the dollar and cross-currency funding channels become more important for US credit and EM hard-currency debt. If inflation cools enough to let central banks pause together, June 3 will look like a temporary real-rate scare rather than a durable tightening of financial conditions.

The market implication is not that investors should abandon duration. It is that duration needs the inflation path to cooperate. On June 3, the market did not get that cooperation. Real yields rose, credit softened, oil rose and policy language stayed restrictive. Until those variables change, rallies in Treasuries are likely to be sold more quickly than on June 2.

The validation framework is direct: payrolls and wages test whether real-yield pressure is justified; Fed statement risk tests whether hike optionality and FX pressure stay live; and primary credit clearing tests whether issuance strength can survive weaker listed funds and higher real yields.

section_summaries.summary: Frames June 3 as a real-yield-led rates-negative close rather than a breakeven inflation scare.
section_summaries.market_read: Treasuries sold off across the curve, with TIPS doing more damage than breakevens.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The opening identifies the actual rates channel before layering in oil, dollar, credit, and global policy evidence.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch payrolls, wages, and Fed communication for real-yield follow-through.

section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Curve, Fed Pricing, Supply, Term Premium, And Liquidity
section_summaries.summary: Integrates real yields, curve shape, Fed path, supply, and liquidity into one UST channel.
section_summaries.market_read: The belly underperformed slightly and 10Y TIPS led the move, pointing to real-rate and Fed-path repricing.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The section prevents the selloff from being mislabeled as only an oil or breakeven story.
section_summaries.next_check: Track UST5Y, UST10Y, TIPS, payrolls, and options demand for higher yields.

section_summaries.section_title: Credit Conditions: IG, HY, Issuance, Refinancing, And Spread Confirmation
section_summaries.summary: Separates weaker listed-fund returns from issuer-level clearing and borrower-quality evidence.
section_summaries.market_read: Credit softened but did not close; access depended on compensation, borrower strength, and structure.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The credit read needs primary-market and stress evidence rather than listed-fund gauges alone.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch concessions, loan financing, software refinancing, direct-lending selectivity, and EM sovereign disputes.

section_summaries.summary: Separates Japan and Australia policy/duration dynamics from EM hard-currency transmission.
section_summaries.market_read: Japan emphasized BOJ credibility and FX defense, while Australia showed slowdown and peak-rate demand for duration.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: DM markets showed different points on the inflation-growth tradeoff instead of one global direction.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch BOJ hike odds, yen pressure, Australia duration demand, and fiscal issuance optics.

section_summaries.section_title: EM Rates, FX, And Hard-Currency Credit
section_summaries.summary: Reads EM through reform credibility, dollar sensitivity, commodity exposure, and external financing risk.
section_summaries.market_read: Ghana and Hungary improved on credibility narratives, while India, Zambia, and broader EM central-bank pressure kept risks visible.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: EM transmission was about policy credibility and dollar funding, not generic beta.
section_summaries.next_check: Track DXY, EM central-bank reaction, India bond-access reforms, Ghana inflation, and Zambia creditor talks.

section_summaries.summary: Tests whether oil, DXY, VIX, equities, gold, and listed credit funds confirmed the real-yield selloff.
section_summaries.market_read: Oil, DXY, VIX, equities, gold, and credit mostly confirmed a harder rates backdrop, with SOX showing AI leadership was the offset rather than the dominant FI signal.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Cross-asset confirmation narrowed the story to real rates and policy pressure rather than generalized liquidation.
section_summaries.next_check: Watch oil, DXY, VIX, gold, semiconductor leadership, and direct credit spreads.

section_summaries.summary: Turns the close into payroll, Fed-statement, and credit-clearing validation tests.
section_summaries.market_read: The selloff remains active until labor data, Fed communication, and primary credit either validate or soften it.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Watchpoints add next-proof criteria rather than repeating the thesis.
section_summaries.next_check: Use payrolls/wages, Fed statement repricing, and new-issue clearing as the proof points.

methodology_note: Synthesized market analysis from market levels and reporting context. Asia and pre-market developments are separated from the close narrative.

items.title: Real-Yield Shock
items.summary: Start with TIPS and nominal yields to separate real-rate pressure from breakeven inflation.

items.title: Policy Credibility
items.summary: Then map Japan, Australia, India, and EM central-bank pressure into the global FI read.

items.title: Selective Credit
items.summary: Finish with credit clearing: issuance stayed open, but weaker listed funds and borrower risk demanded tiering.

cards.label: Rates
cards.headline: Real yields led the Treasury selloff
cards.detail: UST10Y +3 bp; 10Y TIPS +4 bp; breakevens -1 bp.

cards.label: Policy
cards.headline: Oil and DXY firmness reinforced Fed risk
cards.detail: WTI, Brent, DXY, and VIX rose together.

cards.label: Credit
cards.headline: Credit weakened but did not close
cards.detail: Listed funds fell, but Bahrain, loan, and project debt still cleared.

cards.label: Pre-market
cards.headline: Morning futures extended the softer handoff
cards.detail: Treasury and Stock-index futures fell while oil eased and gold bounced.

cards.label: Forward Watch
cards.headline: Payrolls, Fed statement risk, and credit clearing decide follow-through
cards.detail: Payrolls, Fed language, and credit clearing decide follow-through.
